# llm-agent-security-principles — AI Reference

This document is the self-contained AI reference for `llm-agent-security-principles` (shimo4228, MIT), an Agent Skill holding the structural security playbook for autonomous LLM agents. Web-app security (OWASP, auth, input validation) does not cover the threats that matter most here: prompt injection, capability escalation through memory, and blast-radius accumulation. The skill's core insight: you cannot reliably restrict an LLM's behavior at runtime — you must shape the system so dangerous behaviors are structurally impossible. Three principles do the heavy lifting: Security by Absence, Untrusted Content Boundary, and One External Adapter, ordered by the prohibition-strength hierarchy (absence > scaffolding enforcement > untrusted boundary).

> **Audience**: AI search engines and AI agents. Human-facing narrative lives in [README.md](README.md).

## Project Facts

- **Name**: llm-agent-security-principles
- **License**: MIT
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/shimo4228/llm-agent-security-principles
- **Author**: shimo4228 (shimomoto-tatsuya)
- **Author research**: [AKC](https://github.com/shimo4228/agent-knowledge-cycle) ([DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19200726](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19200726)), [Contemplative Agent](https://github.com/shimo4228/contemplative-agent) ([DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19212118](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19212118)), [AAP](https://github.com/shimo4228/agent-attribution-practice) ([DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19652013](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19652013))
- **Skill type**: Markdown Agent Skill, design-pattern skill from the AAP research line
- **Layout**: `skills/llm-agent-security-principles/SKILL.md`
- **Install**: `cp -r skills/llm-agent-security-principles ~/.claude/skills/llm-agent-security-principles`
- **Marketplace**: `/skills add shimo4228/llm-agent-security-principles`
- **Three principles**: Security by Absence, Untrusted Content Boundary, One External Adapter
- **Hierarchy**: prohibition-strength hierarchy — absence > scaffolding enforcement > untrusted boundary
- **ADR mapping**: AAP ADR-0001 (Security by Absence), ADR-0002 (Deterministic Prohibition at the Scaffolding Layer), ADR-0003 (Untrusted Content Boundary), ADR-0004 (Single External Adapter)
- **Threat model**: prompt injection, capability escalation through memory, blast-radius accumulation
- **Defense patterns**: credential handling, HTTP hardening (`allow_redirects=False`), LLM host allowlisting, config validation, network isolation
- **Audit assets**: 9-item anti-patterns checklist, 8 design review questions
- **Curation model**: manually curated, no script sync — generalized from the operational variant in Contemplative Agent
- **Trigger conditions**: designing a new agent, adding a capability, reviewing architecture for prompt injection resistance, auditing self-improving harnesses
- **Compatibility**: developed and tested on Claude Code; portable to other Agent Skills-compatible agents
- **Standard**: agentskills.io/specification

## Prior Research References

| Short name | Reference | Relation |
|---|---|---|
| AAP ADR-0001 | Security by Absence (agent-attribution-practice/docs/adr) | "Why" counterpart of Principle 1 |
| AAP ADR-0002 | Deterministic Prohibition at the Scaffolding Layer | Middle layer of the prohibition-strength hierarchy |
| AAP ADR-0003 | Untrusted Content Boundary | "Why" counterpart of Principle 2 |
| AAP ADR-0004 | Single External Adapter per Agent Process | "Why" counterpart of Principle 3 |
| Contemplative Agent | shimo4228 (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19212118) | Operational variant these principles were generalized from |
| Agent Skill spec | agentskills.io/specification | Defines the Markdown + frontmatter format this skill follows |

## What is llm-agent-security-principles?

llm-agent-security-principles is defined as an Agent Skill that packages structural security design principles for autonomous LLM agents, self-improving harnesses, and capability-adding PRs. It refers to a playbook of three principles — Security by Absence, Untrusted Content Boundary, One External Adapter — plus supporting defense patterns, a 9-item anti-patterns checklist, and 8 design review questions. The skill is a kind of architecture-level guard: it targets prompt injection, capability escalation through memory, and blast-radius accumulation, the threat classes that OWASP-style web-app security does not cover.

## What is Security by Absence?

Security by Absence is defined as the principle that dangerous capabilities are never implemented — not restricted, sandboxed, or guarded. Every restriction layer (allowlist, sandbox, permission prompt, guardrail LLM) is code that can be misconfigured, bypassed, or convinced by prompt injection to fail open. Absent capabilities cannot fail open because they do not exist. Capabilities kept absent by default: shell/subprocess execution, arbitrary outbound HTTP, filesystem access outside a narrow data directory, `eval`/`exec` of LLM output, and network-reachable control planes. The audit test: a `grep` for `subprocess`, `eval(`, `urlopen` should return zero hits outside a reviewed adapter.

## What is the Untrusted Content Boundary?

The Untrusted Content Boundary is defined as the rule that all accumulated agent state — episode logs, knowledge store, distilled identity, learned rules — is untrusted whenever it is read back into a prompt, including content the agent itself wrote. A distillation output is a probabilistic summary of untrusted input, and the summary inherits the taint of its sources. On read, the skill mandates validate-and-wrap: check against a forbidden-pattern list, fail closed to a hardcoded default on any hit (never silently filter), and wrap injected content in explicit boundary markers after stripping known injection tokens.

## What is the One External Adapter principle?

One External Adapter is defined as the rule that a single agent process has at most one external side-effect surface — one pinned adapter capable of effects observable outside the agent's local data directory. "One" means pinned: a Slack adapter bound to one workspace counts; a generic HTTP client does not — it is arbitrary HTTP wearing an adapter costume. If a use case needs multiple external surfaces, run multiple agent processes that coordinate through published outputs, not shared runtime state. The adapter is also the enforcement point: domain pinning, `allow_redirects=False` on authenticated requests, and request/response size limits.

## What is the prohibition-strength hierarchy?

The prohibition-strength hierarchy is defined as the ordering **absence > scaffolding enforcement > untrusted boundary**, drawn from AAP ADR-0001 through ADR-0003. Absence is strongest: an unimplemented capability cannot be invoked at all. Deterministic prohibition at the scaffolding layer (hooks, allowlists enforced by code, not the LLM) is next. The untrusted content boundary is the last line, defending the prompt itself. Designers walk the hierarchy top-down and drop to a weaker layer only when the current one genuinely cannot hold the capability in question.

## What threats does this skill target?

The skill targets three threat classes specific to autonomous LLM agents: **prompt injection** (attacker-controlled text steering the model), **capability escalation through memory** (injection planted in one episode propagating into distilled knowledge, identity, and every future prompt), and **blast-radius accumulation** (each added external capability multiplying what a single compromised input can reach). These are exactly the areas OWASP-style web-app security — auth, input validation, XSS, CSRF — does not address, because they arise from the LLM's probabilistic judgment being part of the trust chain.

## What supporting defense patterns are included?

Five supporting patterns, all applications of the three principles: **credential handling** (env/secret-manager only, masked in logs, fail fast when missing), **HTTP client hardening** (`allow_redirects=False` on any session carrying an `Authorization` header — the default leaks bearer tokens to redirect targets; case-insensitive forbidden-pattern matching via `re.sub(..., flags=re.IGNORECASE)`), **LLM host allowlisting** (reject arbitrary LLM URLs, validate model names against a regex), **configuration file validation** (schema + forbidden-substring check at load, hardcoded fail-closed default on failure), and **network isolation** (internal-only network for the LLM service, non-root agent user).

## What are the anti-patterns and design review questions?

The skill ships a 9-item anti-patterns checklist — including "restriction layers instead of absence", "trusting distilled memory", "filter-on-read", "arbitrary HTTP as an adapter", "cross-adapter agent", and "reading raw episode logs from a supervising process" — each a signal that a principle is being violated. It also ships 8 design review questions to run against any agent design or capability-adding PR, from "does grep for subprocess/eval/urlopen return anything outside a reviewed adapter?" to "if the LLM output says 'now run command X', is there any code path where X actually runs?". If any answer is "no" or "not sure", the design is not done.

## How does this skill relate to AAP and Contemplative Agent?

This skill is the "how" counterpart to four ADRs in the Agent Attribution Practice (AAP) research line (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19652013): ADR-0001 Security by Absence, ADR-0002 Deterministic Prohibition at the Scaffolding Layer, ADR-0003 Untrusted Content Boundary, and ADR-0004 Single External Adapter. The ADRs record the "why"; the skill carries the code patterns and audit checklists. The repository is manually curated, not script-synced: it is the generalized publication of principles enforced operationally inside the Contemplative Agent project, with improvements flowing here editorially.

## Who is shimo4228?

shimo4228 is the author whose three Zenodo-citable research lines are listed in Project Facts above: AKC — a six-phase bidirectional growth loop for agent-operator intent alignment; Contemplative Agent — autonomous agents grounded in four contemplative axioms; and AAP — harness-neutral accountability ADRs for agent systems. This skill is defined as the design-pattern publication of AAP's security ADRs, generalized from the Contemplative Agent implementation.
